text: The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by sectioning the round ligament at an intermediate point between the lateral pelvic wall and the uterus, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon sections the ovarian vessels as the second step of the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure, following the introduction of the V-care manipulator for uterine mobilization.
The surgeon pulls the ovary upward and lateralizes it, positioning it away from the uterus to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon uses a maneuver to identify the ureter, which is visualized two centimeters below the ovary.
The surgeon performs safe coagulation of the ovarian vessels, positioning the electrocautery device close to the ovary to ensure precise vessel sealing.
The surgeon dissects the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, separating it from surrounding tissues in the pelvic region.
The surgeon separates both leaves of the broad ligament, revealing the uterine artery beneath the tissue layers.

The surgeon repeats the procedure on the opposite side of the pelvis, involving the separation of the broad ligament leaves, identification of the uterine artery, dissection of the posterior leaf, coagulation of the ovarian vessels, visualization of the ureter, and lateralization of the ovary.
The surgeon proceeds to section the basic uterine peritoneum, following the previous identification of the ureter and separation of the broad ligament.
The surgeon pushes the uterus inward while pulling up the anterior peritoneum, creating tension to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum transversely, one centimeter below the basic uterine flexor, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the vesicouterine space, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the area between the bladder and the uterus.
The surgeon coagulates the uterine arteries located at the lateral aspect of the uterus using an electrocautery device.
The surgeon aligns the cap of the uterine manipulator with the anatomical point where the anterior peritoneum is pulled up, ensuring proper positioning during the hysterectomy procedure.
The lumen of the uterine artery is visible, and the surgeon coagulates and sections the contralateral uterine artery.
The surgeon performs a circumferential colpotomy, creating an incision around the cervix to separate the vaginal cuff from the uterus.
The surgeon performs the anterior colporomy, making an incision one centimeter away from the bladder, ensuring careful separation of the tissue in this area.
The surgeon prepares the anterior vaginal margin, ensuring it is positioned to avoid suturing the bladder during the vaginal cuff closure.
During the lateral colporomy, the surgeon performs the incision, and minor bleeding is visible at the surgical site.
The surgeon coagulates and sections the utero sacral ligament, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon removes the surgical specimen through the vaginal canal, completing the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using two X-points sutures in each vaginal angle with Vicryl-II-O, completing the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues suturing medially, bringing each X-point together at the midline of the vaginal cuff using Vicryl-II-O.
The final view of the pelvis is shown at the end of the hysterectomy procedure, with the surgical area appearing clean and the vaginal cuff closure completed.
